myths.Agent_1:
In the Iron Peaks, during the Great Dearth, Elara sat by a dying fire with her last measure of grain. A knock rattled the door. There stood a traveler, his face hidden by a cowl of shifting shadows. Elara knew that to feed him was to hasten her own end, yet she ushered him in, offering her chair and the final loaf.

Her neighbor, Kael, burst in, eyes wild with hunger. "Why waste bread on a shadow?" he roared, snatching the grain from the stranger’s hand. He moved to cast the traveler back into the blizzard, violating the threshold peace.

The traveler stood, and the rafters groaned. The cowl fell away to reveal eyes that held the genesis of stars. "I am the First Guest," he thundered. "The hearth is not a choice; it is the boundary between man and beast."

Kael withered into a pillar of salt, destined to stand forever at the gate as a warning. The bread in Elara's hand turned to gold, yet the lesson remained etched in the valley’s stone. Hospitality was no longer an act of pity, but a blood-binding ritual. To deny the guest is to unmake the world; the host serves the law, and the law preserves the soul.

myths.Agent_1:
Myth: In the time of Great Shiver, Elara tended the last ember of her ancestral line. A traveler with skin like polished silver and eyes of liquid void sought refuge. To shelter him was to divide her warmth, yet she invited him to the hearth, offering her only cloak. Her brother, Kael, consumed by the frost’s bite, demanded the stranger be cast out. 'Why warm a shadow while our blood freezes?' he spat, reaching for the Guest’s throat to reclaim the heat.

The Guest stood, his presence expanding until the walls of the hut seemed like thin parchment. He was the Weaver of the Threshold, the god who binds host to stars. 'The hearth is not yours to guard,' he thundered, 'it is the bridge between the finite and the eternal.' Kael was not slain, but his warmth was stolen forever; he became a shadow on the wall, a flickering memory of greed. The traveler left behind the Edict of the Ember: 'A guest is the breath of the house. To quench their light is to plunge the soul into the void.' From then on, the law of the hearth was written in fire, a sacred chain never to be broken.

A traveler arrived, his body made of gears and starlight, seeking a single melody from the fruit. To share the song was to quiet her own village, yet Elara offered a golden pear. Kael, her kinsman, his mind a fortress of iron logic, snatched the fruit away. "The song belongs to the gears that keep us turning!" he shouted, crushing the fruit into pulp.

The traveler did not weep; instead, he expanded into a vast storm of celestial harmony. "I am the Conductor of the Unseen Choir," he boomed. "The tree is not a machine to be maintained, but a symphony to be shared. To silence the visitor is to deafen the world." Kael was not shattered, but his voice was replaced by the grinding of rusted metal, a silent witness to the music he despised. The Conductor left the Litany of the Orchard: "The guest is the harmony that completes the chord. To refuse the stranger is to live in silence." From that day, the orchard flourished only when its songs were cast to the four wild winds.
